skill.name: database-migration
skill.description: Plan and review database migrations across ORMs with schema changes, data transformation, rollback procedures, and zero-downtime deployment patterns.
skill.summary: Plan safer database migrations with ORM examples, rollback strategies, and zero-downtime patterns.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: Static external-command, weak-crypto, and system-reconnaissance alerts were false positives from Markdown code fences and ordinary database terminology. The skill has no prompt injection or malicious intent, but it includes migration commands and destructive schema examples, so publication should carry a database safety warning.

medium_findings.title: Destructive database migration examples require review
medium_findings.description: The skill includes legitimate migration examples that drop tables, remove columns, and rebuild tables from backups. These operations can cause data loss if copied into production without review, backups, and staging validation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines directly show destructive schema or table operations. The context is legitimate database migration guidance, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

low_findings.title: False positive: Markdown code fences detected as shell execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer marked many triple-backtick Markdown fences as Ruby or shell backtick execution. These are documentation code blocks, not executable backtick expressions in the skill runtime.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The locations are Markdown fenced examples visible in SKILL.md. I found no Ruby source or runtime shell backtick expression in the scanned file.

low_findings.title: False positive: Weak cryptography alert has no cryptographic context
low_findings.description: The flagged lines are the skill description, a database version-upgrade bullet, and a Markdown reference path. No hash, cipher, signing, or encryption implementation appears at these locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact lines contain database migration prose and a .md reference. There is no evidence of weak cryptographic algorithm use.

low_findings.title: False positive: System reconnaissance alert is database identifier syntax
low_findings.description: The flagged lines use database primary-key and SQL parameter syntax, such as id fields and WHERE id clauses. They do not collect host, process, user, network, or environment information.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are clearly ORM and SQL examples using record identifiers. No system reconnaissance behavior is present in the scanned content.

dangerous_patterns.title: Migration command examples execute project tooling
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents npx and npm migration commands. These are normal for ORM migrations, but users should run them only in trusted projects and staged environments.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are explicit and relevant to the skill. They are not malicious, but they can trigger database changes when run.

content.value_statement: Database changes can break applications or destroy data when they are rushed. This skill helps plan ORM migrations, rollbacks, data transforms, and zero-downtime release steps.
actual_capabilities: Provides Sequelize, TypeORM, and Prisma migration examples.
Explains schema transformation patterns for columns and data types.
Shows data backfill and record transformation approaches.
Describes transaction-based and checkpoint rollback strategies.
Outlines blue-green steps for zero-downtime database changes.
Highlights cross-database type differences for PostgreSQL and MySQL.
limitations: Does not inspect a live database or verify production state by itself.
Examples must be adapted to each schema, ORM version, and database engine.
Destructive operations still require backups, staging tests, and human approval.
Referenced support files are listed but not present in this skill package.

use_cases.title: Prepare an ORM Schema Change
use_cases.description: Create an ordered migration plan for adding, renaming, or removing columns while preserving rollback options.

use_cases.title: Reduce Release Downtime
use_cases.description: Plan multi-phase database changes that keep old and new application versions compatible during deployment.

use_cases.title: Review Migration Risk
use_cases.description: Check a proposed data migration for backup needs, transaction boundaries, rollback gaps, and large-table concerns.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Migration
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a simple ORM migration.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create a database migration for this schema change: [describe change]. Use [Sequelize, TypeORM, or Prisma] and include a rollback.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Safe Column Rename
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request for backward-compatible schema evolution.
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a zero-downtime column rename from [old column] to [new column] on [table]. Include application deployment phases and rollback steps.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Data Backfill
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate review of a migration that transforms existing rows.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this data migration plan for safety: [paste plan]. Identify transaction, batching, locking, null handling, and rollback risks.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Cross-Database Migration
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced migration across database engines or ORM platforms.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a migration from [source database or ORM] to [target database or ORM]. Cover type mapping, data validation, cutover, rollback, and monitoring.

output_examples.input: Plan a zero-downtime rename of users.name to users.full_name.
output: Deploy code that writes both fields.
Switch reads to the new field.
Remove the old column after validation.

output_examples.input: Review a migration that drops users.address_string after splitting it into separate fields.
output: Validate parsing for missing address parts.
Run the change on staging first.

output_examples.input: Create a rollback strategy for a risky schema change.
output_examples.output: The response would define a transaction or checkpoint plan, verification checks, restore steps, and conditions for stopping the rollout.

best_practices: Test every migration and rollback on staging data before production use.
Prefer small, reversible changes with clear verification checks.
Back up data and monitor errors, locks, and runtime during deployment.
anti_patterns: Do not run destructive examples directly against production data.
Do not combine many unrelated schema changes in one migration.
Do not remove old columns before all deployed code stops using them.

faq.question: Does this skill run migrations for me?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. You decide what commands to run in your environment.

faq.question: Which ORMs does it cover?
faq.answer: It includes examples for Sequelize, TypeORM, and Prisma.

faq.question: Can it help with zero-downtime releases?
faq.answer: Yes. It outlines phased changes such as dual writes, backfills, read switches, and cleanup.

faq.question: Can it prevent data loss?
faq.answer: It can highlight safer patterns, but backups, staging tests, and human review remain required.

faq.question: Does it support cross-database migrations?
faq.answer: It provides basic PostgreSQL and MySQL type-handling examples for cross-database planning.

faq.question: What should I provide in a prompt?
faq.answer: Provide the ORM, database engine, current schema, desired change, data volume, and rollback requirements.
